coredata.dc:description: The safety, affordability, and impressive electrochemical performance of many Zn-ion batteries (ZIBs) has recently triggered an overwhelming literature surge. As is typical for a new area, initial enthusiasm and high expectations have now been replaced by a more measured period of research that reaches deep into the underlying factors controlling electrochemical properties. Rather than battery metrics, this review focuses on fundamental aspects of the chemistry of ZIBs that are the least understood and on which there has been progress over the last few years. We provide guidance for future research regarding (1) the significant challenge of proton/Zn2+ co-intercalation in aqueous media, (2) limitations to conversion chemistry that often accompanies ZIB electrochemistry, (3) positive aspects of facile Zn2+ (de)intercalation in nonaqueous electrolytes and organic cathode materials, (4) the desolvation penalty at electrode-electrolyte interfaces, (5) solutions for controlling Zn dendritic growth, and (6) suggested electrochemistry protocols for the field.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Illustration of a Zn-Ion Battery and Topics Addressed in This Review

Left: water-based electrolytes provide superior safety and fast (dis)charge kinetics, whereas non-aqueous solvents traditionally suffer from relatively poor ionic conductivity and large energy penalties at the interface due to increased contact-ion pairs (CIPs). However, the H+/Zn2+ co-insertion —and concomitant precipitation of a Zn LDH—is not reversible after the LDH detaches from the electrode surface. Right: structural comparison of the most successful reported ZIB cathode candidates.
Summary of Current Challenges and Future Directions in ZIB Electrochemistry
Evidence of H+/Zn2+ Co-insertion and LDH Deposition during Cycling of δ-MnO2

Summary of General Performance Metrics for Some Reported Cathode Materials in AZIBs and Growth in the Area

Parameters include relative proportion of AZIB publications for each respective class of material, typical cycle life retaining at least 80% of the initial capacity at slow (<1 A g−1) versus fast (≥1 A g−1) rates (outliers are excluded in this dataset), average discharge capacity (mAh g−1), and operating voltage (V).
(A) Metrics for oxide cathode materials in AZIBs, including Mn-oxides (green), V-oxides (blue), and polyanionic frameworks (cyan).
(B) Metrics for non-oxide cathode materials in AZIBs, including PBAs (purple), organic materials (brown), and sulfides (yellow).

head.ce:title: Scientific Challenges for the Implementation of Zn-Ion Batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safety, affordability, and impressive electrochemical performance of many Zn-ion batteries (ZIBs) has recently triggered an overwhelming literature surge. As is typical for a new area, initial enthusiasm and high expectations have now been replaced by a more measured period of research that reaches deep into the underlying factors controlling electrochemical properties. Rather than battery metrics, this review focuses on fundamental aspects of the chemistry of ZIBs that are the least understood and on which there has been progress over the last few years. We provide guidance for future research regarding (1) the significant challenge of proton/Zn2+ co-intercalation in aqueous media, (2) limitations to conversion chemistry that often accompanies ZIB electrochemistry, (3) positive aspects of facile Zn2+ (de)intercalation in nonaqueous electrolytes and organic cathode materials, (4) the desolvation penalty at electrode-electrolyte interfaces, (5) solutions for controlling Zn dendritic growth, and (6) suggested electrochemistry protocols for the field.

Looming concerns of climate change and the prospects of sustainable energy resources, such as solar and wind, have entered the global spotlight, triggering the hunt for reliable, low-cost electrochemical energy storage. Among the various options, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are currently the most attractive candidates because of their high energy density and foothold in the marketplace. However, many factors (expense, safety, and lifetime) will likely limit their large-scale applications and dictate against their use in stationary electrochemical energy storage (grid, mini-grids, telecommunication stations, etc.) wherein low cost and durability are more of a concern than weight. Alternative battery technologies are thus of considerable current interest. These include Zn-ion batteries (that utilize a Zn metal negative electrode), which offer greater safety, environmental friendliness, and affordability compared with their Li-ion cousins.

There has been an explosion of research in this field over the past few years—resulting in a plethora of publications—but clarity on the fundamental aspects of Zn-battery function in both aqueous and non-aqueous media is yet to be achieved. In this review, we provide a broad yet balanced overview of the field, insights into important progress made to date, key challenges to be overcome in future research, and perspectives on the many open questions remaining. In particular, we critically analyze the problem of proton co-intercalation in aqueous media and address the challenges it imposes on the practical implementation of Zn-ion technology for large-scale applications. We question whether Zn-ion intercalation (i.e., diffusion in the bulk) or pseudocapacitive behavior underlies some ultra-high rates that have been reported in aqueous electrolytes and note that the surprisingly good performance of some cathode materials in non-aqueous cells bodes well for future development.

Zn-ion batteries are frequently cited with promising metrics for a myriad of applications including stationary storage. However, fundamental aspects of their chemistry, which may hinder commercialization, have been largely overlooked in the recent literature. In this review, we focus on these aspects and provide guidance for future research, including the challenge of proton/Zn2+ co-intercalation, fast rate-testing protocols and Zn dendritic growth in aqueous electrolytes, limitations of non-aqueous electrolytes, and an evaluation of potential cathode candidates.

ce:section.ce:para:
Global demand for economical and sustainable energy storage has intensified due to rising environmental concerns and the subsequent need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Batteries are an exceptionally promising technology to minimize dependence on fossil fuels by providing means for electric vehicle (EV) propulsion. 1–4 They could also facilitate the integration of renewable resources into the electric grid by efficiently storing energy harnessed from intermittent power sources. 5
,
6 An ideal battery, composed of inexpensive and eco-friendly materials, would safely deliver a large reversible capacity with good rate capability under a wide variety of operating conditions. However, no product currently available on the market is capable of satisfying all of these criteria simultaneously. Instead, a variety of energy storage devices must be designed to fulfill the specific requirements of various systems. 6
,
7

Owing to their light weight, high energy density, and extended cycle life, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are used to power most portable/rechargeable devices. 1
,
8 However, the flammable nature of their liquid electrolytes and the expense of scarce resources required for their cell design present many safety hazards and economic challenges that render LIB technology less suitable for some applications. 9 Fortunately, zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs) may offer a promising solution to many of these concerns, especially when aqueous electrolytes are considered. The charge storage mechanism within a ZIB relies on the migration of Zn2+ ions between a Zn metal negative electrode (anode) and positive electrode (cathode) material capable of reversible Zn2+ (de)insertion (Figure 1 ). 10 This technology benefits from Zn’s large volumetric capacity (5,851 mAh mL−1), abundance, and superior safety in comparison with lithium and other metals. 11 Even though many alternative multivalent systems could potentially offer large volumetric capacities (Mg, Ca, and Al with 3,833, 2,073, and 8,046 mAh mL−1, respectively), 12 most metal anodes display poor electrochemical performance in the presence of water because of the formation of electrochemically inert byproducts that passivate the electrode surface. To avoid issues with anode passivation/cell failure, aqueous battery researchers often pair novel multivalent cathode materials with a carbon-based anode acting as a supercapacitor, rather than screening electrochemical performance in a metal cell. ZIBs are among the few systems where metal cells have been successfully cycled in both aqueous 10
,
13–15 and non-aqueous 16–18 electrolytes.

Primary Zn batteries pair MnO2 versus Zn in an alkaline electrolyte, and these disposable cells are already commonly used to power most handheld devices. However, such cells are largely irreversible owing to the formation of passivating byproducts at both the anode (ZnO, Zn(OH)2, etc.) and cathode (Mn(OH)2, MnOOH, etc.) interfaces. 10
,
19 Despite their affordable price, these products are obviously inappropriate for large-scale storage, which also requires long-lasting and eco-friendly cell designs and/or materials. Rechargeable ZIBs are capable of meeting these criteria.

In comparison with other prominent energy storage technologies, the safety and affordability offered by ZIBs render them particularly attractive candidates for stationary storage involving the grid or mini-grids and other applications in which gravimetric capacity is not so much a concern (e.g., home batteries and other distributed power sources). Similar to commercially successful alkaline Zn and Zn-air batteries, ZIBs benefit from their use of the Zn metal anode (−0.76 V versus the standard hydrogen electrode), which is environmentally benign, resistant to corrosion, and can reversibly cycle in both aqueous and non-aqueous electrolytes. Furthermore, inexpensive ZnSO4 salts can be used in aqueous ZIB (AZIB) electrolytes that offer (1) superior safety over toxic, corrosive, or flammable solvents 10 and (2) long-term reversibility because Zn dendrite growth and formation of passivating byproducts are largely suppressed in mildly acidic media. 20 Finally, unlike other emerging battery technologies based on abundant and affordable elements (Na, K, Al, etc.), ZIBs can easily be fabricated in open-air environments, which significantly reduces their overall production cost. 21 Despite these advantages, several challenges still need to be addressed before ZIBs can achieve widespread success.

Multivalent technology has been widely investigated in hopes of developing batteries with high volumetric energy densities made possible when multiple electrons can be transferred via (de)insertion of a single ion at the cathode, coupled with a metal anode. However, increased electrostatic interactions inhibit solid-state diffusion, and hence, there is a paucity of electrode materials capable of fast, reversible (de)intercalation of multivalent ions. 12
,
22
,
23 Even though Zn2+ suffers from sluggish diffusion compared to monovalent Li+ or Na+, it typically exhibits enhanced kinetics relative to a “hard” divalent cation, such as Mg2+. 24 In addition to slow diffusion in the solid state, intercalation of multivalent ions can also be impeded by significant desolvation penalties at the electrode-electrolyte interface. All multivalent ions face these large penalties owing to strong Coulombic interactions between the solvated ion and its surrounding solvent shell. 25 For Zn2+, these complications are considerably more substantial in non-aqueous electrolytes owing to increased concentration of contact-ion pairs and a larger resistance to charge transfer across the interface in comparison to aqueous media. 26

Recently, there has been an overwhelming increase in the number of publications related to Zn-based energy storage (as detailed in several comprehensive reviews focusing primarily on performance) 27–35 and/or other Zn technologies (flexible, hybrid, and Zn-air cells), 36–38 which are outside our scope. Despite this wealth of publications, critical chemo-mechanistic aspects of ZIBs—which will impede their commercialization if not overcome—have yet to be characterized and critically discussed. In this review, we focus on those fundamental topics within the field of ZIBs that are the least understood and on which there has been the most progress over the last few years. The focal points highlighted in our review are summarized in Figure 2 ; these include (1) the significant challenge of proton/Zn2+ co-intercalation in aqueous media, (2) limitations to conversion chemistry that often accompanies ZIB electrochemistry, (3) positive aspects of facile Zn2+ intercalation in some spinel oxides in non-aqueous electrolytes, and organic cathode materials (among others), (4) consequences of the desolvation penalty at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces, and (5) factors that control Zn dendrite growth. As with all potentially disruptive battery chemistries, initial enthusiasm and high expectations are usually followed by a more measured period of research that reaches deep into the underlying factors that control electrochemical properties. ZIBs are now in this second phase as described in the following sections that address current challenges for ZIBs concerning cathode materials, electrolytes, and the Zn anode in both aqueous and non-aqueous energy storage systems.

In order for ZIB technology to compete on an industrial scale, cathode materials must be developed that exhibit large reversible (dis)charge capacities and retain those capacities over an extended cycle life. For grid storage applications, these materials should ideally be composed of affordable and environmentally friendly elements. Owing to water and inorganic salts being the lowest cost electrolyte systems, understandably most studies have focused on aqueous batteries, which are reviewed in the following section.

A wide variety of materials have successfully served as positive electrodes in AZIBs. In aqueous conditions, many candidates exhibit impressive electrochemical behavior, offering fast (dis)charge kinetics, excellent rate capability, and extensive cyclability. However, early studies typically focused on characterizing electrochemical performance and rarely provided detailed insight into underlying charge storage mechanisms. Subsequent investigations have demonstrated that a significant amount of Zn2+/proton (H+) co-insertion often takes place when oxide cathodes are cycled in aqueous media, whereas Zn2+ ions alone tend to preferentially insert in non-oxide materials. 39 This difference is likely due to the nature of the interface, which is typically hydroxyl-terminated and hydrated in aqueous media for most oxides and phosphates. Studies encompassing theory and experiment identified the formation of a hydrated interface in the LiFePO4-H2O system, for example, where chemisorbed H2O molecules at the (010) surface complete the dangling bonds and form a bridge that facilitates the Li+ cation desolvation process at the interface. 40 Although such a mechanism is likely operative for many cation insertion processes in aqueous media, in the case of divalent cations such as Zn2+, the solvent shell is more tightly bound. Thus, easing that desolvation process is expected to be a critical aspect in controlling the competition between Zn2+ and H+ intercalation at the interface.

Manganese oxide (MnO2) materials are particularly desirable candidates for large-scale manufacturing owing to their abundance, affordability, easy processing, and eco-friendly nature. The parasitic reactions that passivate electrodes in commercialized alkaline (primary) cells are suppressed in the mildly acidic media used in AZIBs, so that MnO2 cathodes ought to be capable of reversible electrochemistry under these conditions. Thus, MnO2 was a logical cathode candidate in the first demonstration of a full-cell AZIB, which exhibited reversible behavior and has been thoroughly investigated since. 41 The charge storage mechanism in Zn||MnO2 cells relies on conversion chemistry, so that the active material repeatedly undergoes significant structural transformations during cycling. The stress and strain induced by such conversion-based processes can promote electrode detachment from current collectors and diminish electrochemical performance. Therefore—despite the economic advantages of Mn-based materials—several other transition metal oxides have been investigated as ZIB cathodes. For example, a large number of V-based candidates exhibited promising performance and were identified as intercalation materials. Because intercalation mechanisms largely leave the host structure intact during cation ingress/egress, V-oxide cathodes frequently display excellent capacity retention and cyclability. However, in many (or most) of the aqueous batteries based on these oxide materials, Zn2+ intercalation is also accompanied by proton intercalation and concomitant deposition of Zn4(OH)6SO4·xH2O (a layered double hydroxide, or LDH) on the particle surface. Following is a discussion of the most investigated oxide materials, with this phenomenon in mind (which is discussed further in Positive Electrode Materials).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conversion Oxides: Manganese- and Cobalt-Based Materials
ce:section.ce:para:
In most Zn||MnO2 cells, the cathode materials undergo conversion reactions on electrochemical reduction, which enables them to achieve large discharge capacities. However, these cells exhibit significant capacity fade over prolonged cycling due to Mn2+ dissolution into the electrolyte 20
,
42
,
43 and structural strain endured by the cathode after repeated transformations. 16
,
44–46 Mn-cathode materials also suffer from low electronic conductivity, which can limit the diffusivity of divalent ions and diminish overall electrode performance. 47 Therefore, a wide variety of techniques (electrolyte additives, 20 structural modifications, 48–50 conductive composites, 51–54 and binder-free electrodes 55–57 ) have been developed to address these issues and enhance the electrochemical properties of Mn-cathode materials in ZIBs.

MnO2 is constructed from MnO6 octahedra that are connected together via edges and/or corners to give different crystallographic polymorphs, depending on the linkage. 58 These tunnel (α-, β-, γ-, and todorokite-), 59–66 layered (δ-), 67–69 and spinel (λ-) 70–72 MnO2 structures share the same fundamental building blocks and can easily transform between the various configurations, enabling reversible conversion chemistry during electrochemical cycling. Detailed structural descriptions of each MnO2 polymorph (along with corresponding electrochemical performance metrics) have already been delineated in previous reviews, 27 so this section will focus on key challenges remaining in the field that hinder the commercialization of aqueous Zn||MnO2 cells: (1) competition between Mn2+ dissolution and MnO2 deposition, (2) appropriate characterization of cation (de)insertion mechanisms, and (3) performance optimization of cathode materials.

The complex structural transitions responsible for observed capacities in Zn||MnO2 cells are difficult to deconvolute, and early studies often focused on characterizing electrochemical behavior. The rapid capacity fade observed in these initial investigations was typically attributed to degradation of active material due to Mn2+ dissolution during cycling. 20 Thus, soluble Mn2+ salts are typically employed as electrolyte additives to inhibit this dissolution and stabilize cycle life. However, electrolytes containing Mn2+ allow for electrodeposition of MnO2 onto the electrode surface, 73 which risks diminishing the quality of the cathode material and/or passivating the electrode-electrolyte interface. Because a delicate balance exists between Mn2+ dissolution into the electrolyte and MnO2 deposition onto the electrode surface, future research is urgently required to provide crucial insight into this equilibrium process.

Recent mechanistic investigations have confirmed that protons play a significant role in the electrochemical behavior and frequently co-insert with Zn2+ ions in a variety of Mn polymorphs. 74–76 One of the first papers to demonstrate this behavior was published by Wang et al. 50 A polymer-strengthened MnO2 cathode was prepared by intercalating polyaniline between δ-MnO2 layers (Figure 3 A); this material exhibited excellent capacity retention, maintaining 125 mAh g−1 for 5,000 cycles at 2 A g−1. However, the process responsible for much of the observed capacity was attributed to proton intercalation and Zn2+ (as the hydroxysulfate salt) reversibly precipitated onto the surface of the active material in addition to inserting between the layers. This behavior was characterized by ex situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Figures 3B and 3C), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging (Figures 3D–3I). Such Zn2+/H+ co-insertion processes are frequently reported in AZIBs using inexpensive ZnSO4 electrolytes suitable for large-scale energy storage applications. Although reports on AZIBs using Zn trifluoromethanesulfonate (OTf) electrolytes do not typically mention H+ intercalation as a competing charge storage mechanism, it is unclear whether the cells truly function as a ZIB or—more likely—if the Zn hydroxytriflate precipitate formed after H+ insertion is not easily detected by diffraction and is thus often underreported (see discussion in Positive Electrode Materials).

A wide variety of techniques have been used to develop electrode composites with increased structural stability and improved electronic conductivity in order to significantly enhance MnO2 electrochemical performance. Other methods—such as elemental doping and/or nano-sizing active materials—can promote Zn2+ intercalation, even in MnO2 polymorphs with constricted pathways for diffusion of the divalent cation. 77–79 However, most studies use a combination of design strategies to prepare electrode materials. Therefore, it is unclear to what degree the electrochemical activity of MnO2 materials is influenced by (1) increased electrode/electrolyte interaction using nano-sized/mesoporous structures, (2) facile diffusion pathways through cation deficiencies, and/or (3) enhanced electrical conductivity/structural stability with conductive composites. Further research will be needed to discriminate between these factors and determine the best strategies to optimize the electrochemical system.

Overall, MnO2 polymorphs provide a wide variety of potential cathode candidates that have demonstrated reversible electrochemistry in aqueous and non-aqueous ZIBs. Nonetheless, there is a large discrepancy regarding the discharged products and insertion mechanisms responsible for the observed capacity in these cells. Several studies report that Zn2+ intercalation initiates structural transformation to spinel ZnMn2O4 and/or layered ZnxMnO2 depending on the depth of discharge. 21
,
44 Other investigations have found that most of the observed capacity should actually be attributed to proton intercalation, as is evidenced by limited observable capacity in dry organic electrolytes and the formation of protonated MnOOH products upon discharge. 20
,
59 For both Zn2+ and H+ insertion mechanisms, an LDH often reversibly precipitates on the electrode’s surface when the pH increases as a result of H+ intercalation and/or Mn2+ dissolution from the cathode. 66
,
80 These discrepancies among reports are often attributed to variations in the active material’s particle size, morphology, etc., but a comprehensive understanding of the principles governing MnO2 electrochemistry has not yet been achieved. Large initial discharge capacities (>250 mAh g−1) have been reported at moderate current densities regardless of the insertion mechanism; however, in most cases better rate capability and capacity retention upon cycling are observed for mechanisms involving H+ intercalation compared with those limited to Zn2+ insertion alone. 43
,
50

In addition to Zn||MnO2 cells, several cobalt oxide cathodes have also achieved large discharge capacities through conversion chemistry. However, most reported Co3O4 spinel electrodes have been developed using alkaline electrolytes and undergo markedly different electrochemistry compared with processes that take place in mildly acidic conditions. 81 In 2018, Ma et al. reported the first ZIB employing a Co(III) rich-Co3O4 cathode with this aqueous electrolyte (2 M ZnSO4 + 0.2 M CoSO4). 82 Similar to most Zn||MnO2 cells, this electrolyte contained a CoSO4 additive to suppress Co2+ dissolution on discharge and prolong cycle life, with 92% capacity retention after 5,000 cycles. Transitioning from Mn to Co increased the potential of the cell to 2.2 V so that this system offers a high energy density of 361 Wh kg−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. Although this work shows great promise for developing reversible AZIBs with >2 V, the use of an expensive metal such as Co hinders its ability to be implemented for grid storage applications.

Since the first report of reversible, high-capacity Zn-ion intercalation, a plethora of V-based cathode materials have been reported to undergo intercalation reactions that enable them to achieve large discharge capacities with excellent Coulombic efficiency. Below, selected reports outlining the behavior of a range of vanadium oxides as the cathode in AZIBs are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pillared Layered XV2O5 (X = Zn, Mg, Ca, Na....)
ce:section.ce:para: Our study was the first to report a vanadium oxide bronze pillared by interlayer Zn2+ ions and water—Zn0.25V2O5⋅nH2O—as the positive electrode for a Zn-ion intercalation cell (Figure 4 ). 13 A reversible Zn2+ ion (de)intercalation storage process with more than one Zn2+ per formula unit (a capacity up to 300 mAh g−1) was achieved at very good rates. The cell’s promising performance was enabled in large part by the nano-dimensions of the cathode in the Zn-ion transport direction (flat fibers ca. 250 × 50 nm), micron-long fibers in the third dimension that provided good electronic transport and continuity, and the ability of the structure to incorporate an additional monolayer of water between the layers upon immersion in the electrolyte. Zn2+ intercalation from its water-solvated electrolyte environment was thus facilitated, because the requirement for desolvation at the interface was minimized. The water was expelled on full Zn2+ intercalation (which decreased the interlayer d-spacing) and resorbed upon de-intercalation (Figure 4A). This Zn cell provided an estimated energy density of ∼450 Wh L−1 (active materials only) and exhibited a capacity retention of more than 80% over 1,000 cycles, with no dendrite formation at the Zn electrode.

This work inspired explorations of analogous layered V2O5 bronze materials with a range of pillaring cations, such as Mg2+, 83 Ca2+, 84 NH4+, 85 and Co2+. 86 All display very similar electrochemical performance as the Zn2+ pillared analog (Figures 4B and 4C), with the exception of Co0.247V2O5∙0.944 H2O, in which a higher average voltage (>1 V versus Zn) was explained by a unique electronic structure resulting from Co-3d, V-3d, and O-2p hybridization (Figure 4D). 86

The exemplary performance of pillared V2O5 cathode materials instigated studies of vanadium oxides of diverse compositions, crystal structures, and morphologies for aqueous Zn2+ storage. In a very short span of time, several candidates have been identified: V5O12·6H2O, V3O7·H2O, V10O24·12H2O, LiV3O8, NaV3O8∙1.5H2O, VO2(B) NaCa0.6V6O16·3H2O, Zn3V2O7(OH)2·2H2O, and Zn2(OH)VO4, to name a few. 26
,
87–96 Fundamentally, all these compounds are very similar with vanadium in a 5+ or 4+/5+ mixed oxidation state and contain either a large interlayer space or open tunnels for divalent Zn2+ intercalation. Naturally, their electrochemical behavior is closely comparable, exhibiting an average voltage of 0.6–1 V with very high capacities, excellent rate capability, and stable cycling at high current rates. Nonetheless, the relatively low discharge potentials of these materials ultimately present a limitation for applications that demand high energy density (i.e., high voltage and large capacity) such as electric mobility. Cycling at very high current rates, especially for nanostructured materials, is often misleadingly assigned to intercalative processes, whereas non-diffusion-limited (capacitive) contributions are expected to dominate under these conditions. Although nano-belt, -rod, and/or -fiber morphologies of V-oxides (typically obtained through the hydrothermal route) have primarily been explored, morphological variations such as porous nanofibers 97 and microspheres 98 obtained by electrospinning and spray-drying methods, respectively, have also received attention. Irrespective of morphology, all the nanostructures were shown to facilitate better electrochemical kinetics due to improved Zn2+ and electron transport based on the nanoscale dimensions.

Very recently, a rocksalt vanadium oxynitride was reported that delivers a high reversible capacity of 603 mAh g−1 at a 0.2C rate, with a very high rate capability. 99 The nitrogen-rich oxynitride (VN0.9O0.15) was claimed to irreversibly oxidize to an oxygen-rich disordered rock salt VN0.2O2.1 during the initial charge to 1.7 V, and cycled reversibly afterward through (de)intercalation of Zn2+ in the interstices. Unusually, no change was observed in the X-ray pattern of the cubic phase, and only peaks corresponding to the triflate LDH reversibly appeared (disappeared) during discharge (charge), suggesting that proton intercalation may be highly dominant and responsible for the ultra-high rate capability. One critical issue that is common to all vanadium oxide materials—poor capacity retention at low current densities—was not addressed. The problem may have its origin in structural disintegration at high degrees of cation intercalation (high capacities) and/or slow vanadium dissolution in mildly acidic electrolytes. Therefore, efforts must be made to identify the true cause of these limitations and devise solutions to realize the full potential of vanadium-oxide cathode materials for AZIBs.

A few publications to date have reported on the promise of Zn2+ insertion in polyanionic vanadium phosphate lattices, unlocking the exciting possibility of higher voltage cathode materials. However, the location and quantity of the Zn2+ inserted in the frameworks after discharge was not disclosed in early studies, nor was the possibility of H+ co-intercalation addressed. The first report focused on utilization of graphene-wrapped NaSICON-like Na3V2(PO4)3 as the cathode material, paired with Zn metal in an aqueous cell with Zn acetate as the salt. 100 Two sodium cations were first extracted on charge in the cell to form NaV2(PO4)3, and subsequently a capacity of 97 mAh.g−1 was obtained on discharge. The authors proposed that xZn2+/2xe− were reversibly cycled with low polarization at an average voltage of 1.15 V versus Zn/Zn2+ at a 0.5C rate. The cell retained 74% capacity after 100 cycles, suggesting the possibility of mechanical and interfacial degradation on ion extraction and/or insertion due to the large size difference of Na+ and Zn2+. Subsequent studies suggest the reversible electrochemical behavior of NaV2(PO4)3 is based on a quasi-two-stage intercalation process that involves both Na+ and Zn2+. 101
,
102 Investigations of Zn2+ insertion in monoclinic LiV2(PO4)3 employed a similar tactic of in situ electrochemical extraction of Li+, followed by cycling the cathode in a 4 mol kg−1 (m, molality) Zn(OTf)2 aqueous electrolyte, which has an anodic stability limit of ∼2.0 V versus Zn. 103 Demonstration of Zn2+ (de)insertion in the lattice was based on XANES spectroscopy that showed changes in the V spectra consistent with vanadium redox processes. The high operating potential of this system (1.7 V versus Zn/Zn2+) was coupled with excellent longevity (4,000 cycles) and surprisingly high rate capability. The latter was attributed to delocalization of the nominal bivalence of the inserted Zn-ion through the p-d hybridization between the V-d and O-p orbitals, thus reducing the effective valence of the Zn from 2 to 1.4. As the Zn2+ presumably inserts as an un-solvated cation, follow-up studies to determine the nature of desolvation at the interface and the mechanistic details of Zn insertion and/or diffusion are warranted.

Only a few non-oxide chemistries have been shown to reversibly host Zn2+ ions. Prussian blue analogs (PBAs) have been extensively researched because their tunnel structure allows for rapid ion migration and favors excellent mechanical stability upon cycling, which allows these cathodes to retain a large percentage of their initial discharge capacity even after hundreds of cycles. 104
,
105 Although their specific capacities are generally lower than those of other potential candidates, in the recent past some PBAs have been engineered to deliver capacities exceeding 100 mA g−1, and their high average voltage often compensates the capacity shortfall. Additional successful non-oxide ZIB cathodes have been recently discovered, including layered sulfides and organic materials.

Of the few non-oxide chemistries that have been shown to host Zn2+ reversibly, metal hexacyanoferrates (MHCFs) are among the most promising. The PBAs of MHCFs are a versatile metal ion host for next-generation energy storage applications due to their open framework structure containing wide intersecting tunnels and interstices for rapid ion migration. 106
,
107 Typically, MHCFs can be formulated as AxMa[Mb(CN)6)]y∙zH2O, where transition metal ions Ma and Mb occupy alternating sites in a rock-salt-type ordered cubic structure with cyanide ions (C≡N−) serving as the bridging ligand. A+ cations occupy the cubic interstices, and H2O molecules can fill the vacant [Mb(CN)6]3− sites (when
M a
M b
> 1) or the empty interstitial cages in the cubic structure (Figure 5 A). The large (4.6 Å wide) cubic interstices can accommodate a variety of ions, even in their solvated form, and therefore, MHCFs have been widely explored for aqueous and non-aqueous metal ion batteries in the past 5 years. 104
,
108 The first AZIB utilizing an MHCF cathode was reported by Zhang et al. for Zn3[Fe(CN)6]]2, which shows a capacity of 60 mAh g−1 at an average voltage of 1.73 V corresponding to one Zn2+ intercalation per unit formula. 105 To date, this is the highest voltage reported for an intercalation type AZIB. Nearly 80% capacity retention is observed after 100 charge-discharge cycles with very high rate capability, but the Coulombic efficiency is rather poor—95%—which is not surprising considering the high charging voltage (2 V) that may lead to the oxidation of the aqueous electrolyte. Later, Liu et al. found that the electrochemical performance of Zn3[Fe(CN)6)]2 correlates strongly with particle morphology. 109 They studied particles with exclusively cubooctahedral, truncated octahedral, and octahedral shapes. All the polyhedral shapes were found to be entirely bound by {100} and/or {111} facets, and the cubooctahedral material with highest {100} surface coverage showed the best rate performance and cycling stability. It was suggested that the different surface structures closely influence the Zn2+ diffusion and active material dissolution, leading to varying electrochemical behavior with different morphology.

The copper hexacyanoferrate of nominal formula KCu[Fe(CN)6] was the second MHCF to be reported by La Mantia et al. and showed similar electrochemical behavior to Zn3[Fe(CN)6)]2 for Zn2+ storage: a very high operating voltage of ∼1.7 V with 60 mAh g−1 capacity at a 1C rate. 14 However, the copper compound retained >95% of the initial capacity after 100 cycles. It is important to note that neither Zn3[Fe(CN)6)]2 nor KCu[Fe(CN)6] has the Prussian blue cubic structure. Drying the as-synthesized hydrated cubic phase above 80°C removes the coordinated water from the vacant [Mb(CN)6]3− sites, which changes the Zn (or Cu) coordination from octahedral ZnN4O2 to tetrahedral ZnN4, and results in a transformation to a rhombohedral (Zn) or hexagonal (Cu) phase. 105 The non-cubic phases were utilized in both studies as the cubic analog is more soluble in aqueous electrolytes. 110 However, upon immersion in the electrolyte, the non-cubic phases slowly transform back to the cubic phase, which can be delayed by addition of excess Zn2+ to the electrolyte. This results in considerable enhancement of the cycling stability. It was further found that the nature of the counteranion of Zn2+ and particularly the Zn2+ concentration in the electrolyte can influence the stability of the copper hexacyanoferrate. A higher concentration of Zn2+ accelerates a phase-transformation-mediated aging process, negatively affecting the stability of the cathode host. 111 Later, the Cui group generally described that copper hexacyanoferrates of nominal formula K0.03Cu[Fe(CN)6]0.65·2.6H2O can reversibly store not just Zn2+ but other divalent and trivalent ions, such as Pb3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Y3+, La3+, etc., from aqueous electrolytes, opening up the possibility for these MHCFs to be used in multivalent ion batteries (Figures 5B and 5C). 112

The number of Zn2+ ions that both the MHCFs discussed above can store is dictated by the electron transfer for the Fe3+/Fe2+ redox, which is limited to ∼0.6 e− (i.e., 0.3 Zn2+). In addition, Zn or Cu that are already present in the starting material add to the dead weight, leading to poor specific capacities. Therefore, an ideal strategy is to start with an MHCF with redox active M2+ at both the Ma and Mb sites so that a 2e− reaction corresponding to two M2+/M3+ can give rise to higher capacity. Na2MnFe(CN)6 embodies this concept and delivers a high specific capacity of 140 mAh g−1 at an energy density of 170 Wh kg−1 in a Na-Zn hybrid aqueous battery with a Zn anode. 113 Addition of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) improves the anodic stability of the aqueous electrolyte and suppresses active material dissolution, rendering 2,000 cycles with 75% capacity retention. Although Zn2+ intercalation was not explored for Na2MnFe(CN)6, the performance would most likely be inferior due to slow diffusion kinetics of Zn2+ compared with that of Na+. In very recent interesting work, Zhi et al. showed that scanning FeFe(CN)6 to a high voltage of 2.3 V (against Zn in a highly concentrated Li-Zn hydrogel electrolyte) activates the low spin Fe(III) coordinated to the C end of the cyano group, which leads to a capacity of ∼75 mAh g−1, improved cycling performance and rate performance, albeit in a rather wide voltage window of 0.01–2.3 V. 114

Transition metal dichalcogenides with layered structures are potential alternatives to layered oxides for divalent cation intercalation, as the higher polarizability of S2− compared with that of O2− offers weaker electrostatics, allowing high ionic mobility. One such material is lamellar VS2 with an interlayer spacing of 5.76 Å, which is large enough to enable cation intercalation with even a solvation sheath. Mai et al. showed that nanosheets of VS2 can store up to ∼0.4 Zn2+ formula unit corresponding to 190 mAh g−1 specific capacity and deliver a stable cycling and good rate performance in a mildly acidic ZnSO4/H2O electrolyte. 115 Yet compared with layered oxide hosts, the energy density is relatively poor due to a lower average voltage (∼0.65 V) and smaller capacity. Kang et al. attempted reversible Zn2+ storage in few other layered materials including sulfides (MoS2, VS2) and borides (TiB2, ZrB2), but none exhibited noteworthy electrochemical performance. 116 However, it is important to note that all these were commercial materials consisting of micrometer-sized particles, which are known to be detrimental for divalent ion diffusion and hence electrochemical reactivity. Therefore, the application of nanostructured materials, so far commonly utilized in all successful studies of (non-)aqueous Zn2+ storage, may prove vital.

Liang et al. demonstrated the importance of such design principles to obtain promising electrochemical performance from seemingly inactive intercalation hosts by tuning the interlayer spacing and intercalation energy of a MoS2 host cathode (Figure 6 A). 117 By lowering the hydrothermal reaction temperature, the authors avoided complete decomposition of the molybdate precursor and produced oxygen-incorporated nanosheets (MoS2–O) with a larger interlayer gallery compared with MoS2 prepared by conventional methods (9.5 versus 6.2 Å, respectively). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the large gallery and presence of oxygen in MoS2–O reduces the number of Zn2+–H2O bonds that must be broken prior to insertion, which significantly lowers the material’s desolvation penalty and facilitates intercalation kinetics. As a result, Zn2+ diffusivity is boosted by three orders of magnitude in MoS2–O, and the cathode exhibits a large 232 mAh g−1 capacity, ten times that of MoS2 (Figure 6B). The intercalation mechanism of MoS2–O is clearly affected by the presence of oxygen in the nanosheets, as the precipitation of LDH was observed at the end of discharge (Figures 6C and 6D). This suggests that proton intercalation may be responsible for some of the observed capacity, which is a phenomenon that is rarely observed for sulfide materials. Thus, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to distinguish between Zn2+ intercalated between the layers and those present in the LDH (after H+ insertion). Unlike oxide materials cycled in aqueous media where H+ co-insertion can dominate electrochemical activity, XPS revealed a ratio of intercalated Zn2+ to Mo of ∼0.62, which corresponds to a capacity of 208 mAh g−1 and confirms that approximately 90% of the observed activity can be attributed to Zn2+ intercalation (Figure 6E).

Recently, organic quinones have been introduced as an alternative to inorganic intercalation materials for AZIBs. Their soft molecular structure held together by weak van der Waals forces allows rapid diffusion of hard divalent cations, which can be accommodated with minimal volume change through facile molecular reorientation (Figure 7 A). Consequently, calix[4]quinone demonstrates a very high Zn2+ storage capacity of 335 mAh g−1 at a small voltage polarization of 70 mV in a Zn(OTf)2-based aqueous electrolyte. 118 When a Nafion separator is applied to suppress the dissolution of the reduced carbonyl compound (detected from the gradual enhancement of the ultraviolet-visible [UV-vis] spectroscopy absorption peaks of the electrolyte with cycling), 87% of the initial capacity is retained after 1,000 cycles at 500 mA g−1 (Figure 7B). Similarly, tetrachloro-1,4-benzoquinone (or p-chloranil) exhibits a high energy density of 200 Wh kg−1 with a remarkable energy efficiency of 95% in an AZIB. 15 Twisted rotation of the p-chloranil molecular columns in the structure accompany the reversible storage of the Zn2+ ions with a very small volume change (−2.7%) leading to a small voltage polarization (50 mV). Even though the quinone species do not undergo dissolution during electrochemical cycling, an uncontrolled phase evolution leads to rapid capacity fading; however, this was suppressed by confining the quinone inside the mesoporous nanochannels of CMK3 carbon to ensure improved electrochemical cyclability. The superior performance of organic compounds in Zn batteries was also reported by Schubert et al., who demonstrated an AZIB with 10,000 cycles with only 14% capacity fading using a redox active organic polymer (9,10-di(1,3-dithiol-2-ylidene)-9,10-dihydroanthracene) as the cathode (Figure 7C). 119 This impressive cycling behavior was ascribed to the stability and insolubility of the oxidized polymer in aqueous electrolytes, which is not the case with non-aqueous solvents. The only drawback was that a relatively large amount (>50 %) of conductive additive was needed to ensure good performance. Nonetheless, the excellent Zn2+ storage performance exhibited by organic solids warrant their further exploration as sustainable and greener options to inorganic hosts in AZIBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of AZIBs: Critical Factors Driving Zn2+ versus H+ Intercalation
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, Zn2+ intercalation is accompanied by proton intercalation and concomitant deposition of Zn4(OH)6SO4·xH2O (LDH) for many oxides (Figure 8 A). Although this buffering mechanism is vital for the reversible operation of AZIBs, it comes with several drawbacks. The LDH is often not identified or acknowledged, owing to its major reflection in XRD patterns at fairly low angle (∼8°). 39 In electrolyte solutions utilizing Zn(OTf)2 or Zn(CH3COO)2 as the salt, the presence of the LDH is frequently missed because the corresponding triflate or acetate salt appears at even lower angle. 120 Therefore, the capacities of many systems for “true Zn2+” intercalation are likely overestimated because proton intercalation can account for at least 50% of the electrochemical capacity. Finally, the LDH delaminates and detaches from the surface of cathode materials when cells are rested in a discharged state. This causes irreversible electrochemical behavior because the pH of the system is not buffered during H+ egress without the presence of the LDH at the electrode-electrolyte interface (Figures 8B–8G). Thus, it is easy to observe promising electrochemical performance during repeated cycling at fast rates, but it is difficult to achieve comparable behavior under realistic conditions required for commercial applications (range of current densities, rest periods between charge and discharge, etc.) Noteworthy is that while most oxides are dominant proton intercalation materials, the non-oxides (such as sulfides and Prussian blues) undergo exclusive Zn2+ storage. This intriguing difference stems from the surface hydroxyl groups on the oxides in aqueous electrolytes, which appear to facilitate proton transfer across the electrode interface. Reversible proton (de)intercalation in oxides leads to reversible (dis)appearance of the LDH precipitate on the cathode surface, formed by the reaction of OH− (which remains after H+ intercalation) with Zn2+ and the electrolyte anion.

Considering that the concentration of Zn2+ is more than 103 times greater than that of H+ in mildly acidic aqueous electrolytes (typical pH ∼4), it is unusual to expect that H+ would be involved in the faradic charge storage process. Yet the reversible (dis)appearance of LDH (see section on Positive Electrode Materials) X-ray reflections during the (dis)charge of oxide-based cathode materials clearly suggests that the faradic reduction and/or oxidation of transition metals in oxides has significant contribution due to H+ (de)intercalation. From a thermodynamic point of view, the intercalation of monovalent H+ would be more favorable than intercalation of divalent Zn2+ due to the large energy penalty associated with Zn2+ desolvation and its stronger Coulombic interaction with the surrounding lattice. Although solvated ion intercalation from aqueous electrolytes is not unlikely, our aqueous versus non-aqueous electrochemical study on V3O7·H2O (see Aqueous versus Non-aqueous Solvents)—which showed identical structural transformation for an equivalent amount of charge transfer—suggests otherwise. 26 Nevertheless, broad thermodynamic consideration must be valid for oxides and non-oxides equally, which is not the case as oxides tend to undergo dominant H+ intercalation, and non-oxides display exclusive Zn2+ intercalation from aqueous electrolytes. Clearly, some other factor is at play. As we discovered earlier through a preliminary molecular dynamics simulation study, H+ intercalation in oxides is a surface-initiated process. 39 The oxide ions of the host lattice and the predominantly hydroxyl terminated surface can stabilize protons in close proximity to the electrode surface through hydrogen bonding and facilitate H+ intercalation. Lack of hydrogen-bonding-mediated surface reconstruction for non-oxides disfavors H+ intercalation. Thus, we can conclude that the kinetics of charge transfer across the electrode-electrolyte interface (not thermodynamics) dictates the nature of faradic charge storage (H+ versus Zn2+) in aqueous electrolytes.

Although aqueous Zn battery electrolytes offer attractive cost, safety features, and very high conductivities that afford rapid (dis)charge rates, their energy density is nonetheless limited by the narrow electrochemical window of the water-based electrolyte (1.2 V). Water-in-salt electrolytes (WISEs) have the potential to increase that window significantly, but obtaining sufficiently high concentrations of Zn salts in water is very challenging. A hybrid cell has been reported that utilizes 1 m Zn bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonamide (TFSI) + 20 m LiTFSI as the electrolyte, Zn as the anode, and a spinel LiMn2O4/Mn2O4 couple at the cathode. 121 Although the electrolyte functions as a third electrode in this configuration (and Li+ (de)intercalation governs the process at the cathode), the arrangement does provide for a Zn anode with a reasonably large electrochemical window. Zn anodes can also be used in non-aqueous electrolyte cells, presenting the benefit of delivering higher volumetric and gravimetric capacities (5,851 mAh mL−1 and 820 mAh g−1, respectively) than those for the graphite anode materials used in Li-ion batteries (756 mAh mL−1 and 372 mAh g−1). The use of Zn anodes also brings other advantages including high natural abundance, a non-existent or manageable solid-electrolyte interface in non-aqueous cells compared with that in the alkali metals (owing to less reducing potential of Zn), and limited dendritic growth. Non-aqueous electrolytes for Zn exist with electrochemical windows reaching up to ∼3.5 V in acetonitrile (ACN) (i.e., Zn(TFSI)2/ACN and Zn(OTF)2/ACN). There is also fascinating science to be explored, including the question of whether the higher polarizability of the d10 configuration of Zn2+ permits better diffusivity in the solid state compared with that of its divalent cousins, Mg2+ and Ca2+. The following section discusses these points in the context of non-aqueous Zn batteries.

One of the first demonstrations of a rechargeable non-aqueous ZIB (NZIB) paired a bilayered hydrated V2O5 cathode (BL-V2O5) versus Zn using 0.5 M Zn(TFSI)2 in ACN as the electrolyte. 122 This system delivers 170 mAh g−1 at 0.85 V; however, its rate capability and cyclability were significantly limited compared with those of layered vanadium oxides cycled in aqueous media. Interestingly, ex situ XRD revealed that the interlayer spacing of the cathode material increased after discharge (unlike most systems where Zn2+ intercalation displaces intercalated water and/or shields repulsion between negatively charged oxide layers so that interlayer spacing decreases after Zn2+ insertion). At 13.5 Å, the d-spacing of the BL-V2O5 cathode is relatively large so that this phenomenon may be attributed to solvent co-intercalation along with Zn2+ insertion. However, Raman spectroscopy measurements of discharged electrodes provided no evidence of ACN intercalated within the V2O5 layers. Thus, the structural changes taking place within this material upon (dis)charge merit further investigation.

Using a non-aqueous 0.5 M Zn(TFSI)2/ACN electrolyte, Han et al. showed that δ-MnO2 had a maximum discharge capacity of 123 mAh g−1 at a current density of 12.3 mA g−1 and retained a capacity of 60 mAh g−1 after 125 cycles. 16 Detailed mechanistic studies were performed at various states of charge using a combination of several analytical tools, including XRD, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, pair distribution function analysis, and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. In contrast to AZIBs, these probes indicate that Zn2+ reversibly (de)intercalates between MnO2 layers in this non-aqueous system without significant proton participation. In fact, this ought to increase the cyclability of the δ-MnO2 cathode, which has a notoriously poor cycle life in aqueous systems due to structural instabilities causing conversion to other phases (e.g., ZnMn2O4 spinels). However, gradual capacity fading was observed after only 30 cycles, which was attributed to both electrolyte decomposition and bulk changes in the δ-MnO2 cathode upon prolonged cycling.

Some of the most promising NZIBs to date were developed by Gewirth’s group and utilized ZnCo2O4 spinel cathodes doped with Al, Ni, and/or Mn (Figure 9 A). Unlike un-doped ZnCo2O4, which exhibits limited electrochemistry due to the known instability of the Co2O4 framework, a partially doped Zn[Al0.67Co1.33]O4 cathode delivers 114 mAh g−1 over 100 cycles at a 0.2C rate. 17 A critical factor affecting the success of this system was the addition of 1,4-diazabicyclo[2,2,2]octane (DABCO) to the 0.3 M Zn(OTf)2/ACN electrolyte. This proton sponge additive sustains good cycling performance by suppressing oxidative decomposition of ACN, which otherwise polymerizes and deposits on electrodes, thus passivating their surface and preventing further electrochemical activity. Although the addition of Al3+ helps stabilize the charged Znx[Al0.67Co1.33]O4 lattice, the presence of the electrochemically inert metal limits the maximum theoretical capacity that can be achieved by the spinel cathode. Therefore, performance was improved by instead doping the Co lattice with Ni and Mn, analogous to approaches frequently employed in the development of LIBs. A Zn[Ni1/2Mn1/2Co]O4 cathode delivered a steady discharge capacity of 174 mAh g−1 over 200 cycles with an open circuit voltage of 2.05 V, yielding the highest energy density yet reported for Zn-intercalation cathode material (305 Wh kg−1). 18 Although the electrochemistry of Zn[Ni1/2Mn1/2Co]O4 shows great potential (Figure 9B), unfortunately, these cathode materials are composed of a significant fraction of Co (50% based on the transition metal), which may hinder their application for grid storage.

An NZIB with an MHCF has been recently demonstrated for K0.86Ni[Fe(CN)6]0.95(H2O)0.76, which delivers a specific capacity of 55 mAh g−1 for 0.3 Zn2+ intercalation, the same as that in the aqueous system for Fe3+/Fe2+ redox. 123 The voltage polarization in the organic electrolyte is quite high, and the rate capability is significantly poor compared with that in aqueous electrolytes, suggesting a kinetic limitation in non-aqueous systems.

Finally, Zhang et al. developed a novel NZIB using a pyrite CoS2 cathode material and an electrolyte based on 0.3 M Zn(OTf)2/ACN + 200 ppm DABCO, which offers a maximum discharge capacity of 283 mAh g−1 at 0.05C. 124 Unlike most electrodes where Zn2+ (de)intercalation is mediated by redox active transition metals, this work demonstrated the first known case of anionic redox activity operating in a multivalent system (Figure 9C). XPS measurements of (dis)charged electrodes revealed reversible interconversions between 2S2− (sulfide) and S2 2− (disulfide) but no oxidation state change for Co, which is consistent with findings of previous studies on pyrites in Li/Na-ion batteries (Figures 9D and 9E). As anionic redox centers have lower atomic mass than transition metals, this finding identifies anionic redox-active metal sulfides as potential candidates for high-capacity multivalent batteries. Despite these promising preliminary results, the CoS2 cathode only achieves a fraction of its theoretical capacity (435 mAh g−1) and exhibits steady capacity fade over prolonged cycling. Thus, further work is needed to prevent redox shutting of polysulfide species produced at large depths of discharge and improve electrochemical performance.

Compared with LIBs (or other monovalent cations), the development of electrolytes for divalent battery systems involving Zn2+, Mg2+, or Ca2+ is particularly challenging. Part of the added difficulty stems from the unlikelihood of a singly charged species to form a stable intermediate via one electron transfer during stripping and plating and/or cation (de)insertion from either electrode. Such singly charged species would be highly reactive due to their inherent instability and could lead to a variety of parasitic reactions involving electrolyte decomposition and/or electrode passivation to hinder electrochemical performance. When the electrochemical behavior of Zn versus Mg was investigated in non-aqueous electrolytes, the CV corresponding to metal deposition suggested that Zn undergoes a simple two-electron reduction mechanism, whereas Mg electrodeposits via a more complicated process and requires chemical disproportionation of the active species in solution prior to electron transfer. 125 Thus, compared with other divalent cations, Zn2+ (1) may be less prone to the formation of highly reactive intermediates upon (de)insertion and (2) does not require elevated temperatures to facilitate the kinectics of chemical steps involved in its electrodeposition, providing a major advantage to ZIB technology over other options for beyond-LIB energy storage. Following is a discussion of key factors to be considered in the development of ZIB electrolytes for energy storage devices.

The metallic Zn anode exhibits different (dis)advantages depending on the media from which Zn is stripped and/or deposited. Aqueous batteries are economically more viable because the electrolytes are generally inexpensive, safe, environmentally friendly, and should allow ambient battery assembly. However, organic electrolytes may offer better anodic (oxidative) stability than their aqueous counterparts, making high-voltage (2–3 V) Zn batteries feasible. 13
,
126 Yet one major factor that separates these systems is their kinetics, which can strongly influence the electrochemical performance that is already restrained by sluggish solid-state diffusion of divalent Zn2+. We unraveled this kinetic factor recently in an aqueous versus non-aqueous study of Zn2+ storage in layered V3O7∙H2O, which shows high specific capacity and power in ZnSO4/H2O compared with low capacity and large voltage hysteresis in Zn(OTf)2/ACN (Figure 10 A). 26 Although the solid-state diffusion behavior and consequent structural changes are the same in both systems, the charge transfer resistance (RCT) at the electrode-electrolyte interface is strikingly high in the non-aqueous medium. The non-aqueous RCT was found to be nearly 15 and 100 times that of aqueous values for the V3O7∙H2O working and Zn counter electrode, respectively. Sluggish kinetics in the non-aqueous media was further apparent from high activation energies of 75 kJ mol−1 (V3O7∙H2O) and 223 kJ mol−1 (Zn) for the non-aqueous charge transfer compared with a small activation energy of ∼20 kJ mol−1 for both electrodes in the aqueous media. This difference in the kinetics stems from a large Zn2+ desolvation penalty in the non-aqueous electrolyte in addition to strong cation-anion association. The latter was predicted in a first principles calculation study by Han et al., 126 whereas suppressed electrostatic repulsion between Zn ions by water shielding facilitates their rapid interfacial diffusion in aqueous media. Besides facile desolvation, a hydrated and hydroxylated solid surface in aqueous electrolytes may also provide a coordination environment for transport of Zn2+/H+ across the solid-liquid interface. These conclusions are further corroborated by other NZIB studies, which are generally marked by relatively mediocre electrochemical performance for host materials that otherwise showcase attractive Zn2+ storage performance in aqueous electrolytes.

The Zn salt is also expected to have an influence on the stability and overall electrochemical performance of ZIBs. As, Han et al. showed, with ACN as the solvent, Zn(OTf)2 offers significantly better oxidative stability than Zn(TFSI)2. 126 This aspect is particularly critical for aqueous electrolytes as water has a relatively small stability window of 1.23 V, but the nature of the salt used and its concentration can influence its window dramatically. 127 The solvent-in-salt electrolyte concept that has been exploited in both aqueous and non-aqueous batteries to resolve stability and reversibility issues was recently implemented for Zn cycling. By employing a pH neutral 20 m LiTFSI + 1 m Zn(TFSI)2 salt mixture in water as the electrolyte, Wang et al. demonstrated dendrite-free cycling of a Zn anode with nearly 100% Coulombic efficiency. 121 Molecular dynamics calculations further revealed that in this highly concentrated electrolyte, the TFSI− anion completely eliminates H2O from the Zn2+ solvation shell (Figure 10B), which not only suppresses H2 evolution at low voltages but also prohibits O2 evolution with a high-voltage cathode such as LiMn2O4. Nonetheless, the large voltage polarization observed with the concentrated electrolyte can be a concerning factor. At a small current density of 0.2 mA cm−2, with a very small cycling capacity of ∼0.03 mAh, the hysteresis between the sloping Zn plating and stripping profiles is around 200–300 mV. Furthermore, Li/Zn salts with fluorinated anions are expensive, potentially compromising the economic advantage of using inexpensive water-based electrolytes. Renewable and grid scale storage demand cost effective AZIBs, and the use of inexpensive salts such as ZnSO4 is thus vital. The electrolyte stability regime can also be modified by using inexpensive additives such as SDS that has been shown to expand the Na2SO4 + ZnSO4/H2O electrolyte voltage window by nearly 0.5 V (see Prussian Blue Analogs). 113

Zn has been widely used as an anode in different types of batteries, but historically the development of Zn-based batteries has been limited by their poor reversibility and morphological instability, which are a function of current density and the pH of the electrolyte. 128 These issues are typical of alkaline electrolytes 129 and were believed not to be a concern for near-neutral aqueous electrolytes (pH 3–7) until sometime ago. However, issues related to Zn anode cyclability in AZIBs have been acknowledged as of late. 130
,
131 In a recent comprehensive study, we showed that under realistic test conditions (keeping practical applications in mind) the Zn anode cycling is vulnerable to dendritic failure in all common AZIB electrolytes. Large capacities (≥1 mAh cm−2) and rather oddly, low current densities (≤2 mA cm−2) lead to large Zn morphologies; enough to cause shorts across thin separators. 132 In order to address the reversibility (Coulombic efficiency of Zn plating and stripping) and stability (uneven Zn deposition and dendritic short circuits) concerns, primarily four strategies have been evaluated: (1) electrolyte optimization, (2) Zn electrode surface modification, (3) novel Zn host design, and (4) electrochemical protocol development. Triethyl phosphate 133 and polyethyleneimine 134 are some of the most promising electrolyte additives (with the former in a 7:3 ratio with water as the electrolyte solvent (and Zn(OTf)2 salt) that ensure nearly non-dendritic Zn deposition morphology and hence enhanced cycle life with improved Coulombic efficiencies. Zn surface modification by means of surface coating with carbon, 135 graphene oxide, 136 polyamide, 137 CaCO3, 138 and TiO2
139 ; application of 3D Zn hosts based on conducting graphitic fibers, 140 multifunctional carbon nanotubes, 141 and metallic foams 142–144 ; and ingenious electrochemical protocols referred to as “electro-healing” approaches 145 have attained success in mitigating dendritic Zn growth during cycling. Yet fundamentally, these studies are based on a conventional understanding of electrochemical metal plating. 146 Often practical aspects such as current density, stripped and/or deposited capacity, and the inter-electrode distance—all of which can significantly alter the Zn cycling behavior—were not taken into consideration. Attaining long-term Zn cyclability (and thus an extended AZIB cycle life) at both low and high currents with practical capacities and a viable cell configuration is crucial to meet the requirements of many commercial large-scale storage applications. Particular attention must therefore be paid to experimental conditions while evaluating approaches to prolong the Zn cycle life and demonstrating real “breakthroughs” in the development of ZIBs if we are to avoid falling into a “hype cycle.”

Because the bulk of ZIB literature encompasses aqueous systems, we provide a focused discussion on mechanistic challenges of AZIBs in Fundamental Challenges Facing Aqueous Technology. We then recommend testing protocols and performance metrics that should be disclosed in ZIB reports to promote progress within the field. Finally, we suggest some promising directions and strategies for the development of both aqueous and non-aqueous systems.

As described above, a high-volumetric-capacity Zn anode, inexpensive aqueous electrolytes, and the possibility of cost-effective ambient battery assembly are some of the unparalleled advantages of aqueous Zn-ion systems. A synopsis of aqueous Zn-ion technology metrics frequently reported in the literature, along with a measure of growth in the area in recent years, is schematically presented in Figure 11 . Unlike aqueous batteries based on Li/Na/Mg-ions, which are primarily hybrid systems combining an intercalation cathode with a supercapacitor type anode, 147
,
148 a high-energy-density metal anode is possible because the Zn stripping and plating potential is not highly negative. Although the potential is still well below that of the thermodynamic potential for the hydrogen evolution reaction, the latter is associated with a high (kinetic) overpotential, making Zn/Zn2+ redox favorable over H2O reduction, which allows water to be used as an electrolyte. However, a misconception is that Zn dendrite growth is not an issue, when in fact it is (as discussed above), although some new approaches in the field have shown promise in this regard.

With regard to the cathode, side reactions such as active material dissolution, proton co-intercalation, and oxygen evolution at high voltage can pose serious threats to the reversibility and stability of AZIBs. For example, Mn2+ dissolution during α-MnO2 discharge in 1 M ZnSO4 leads to rapid capacity fading. 20 Similarly, MHCFs based on manganese are also prone to dissolution in aqueous electrolytes. 104 Instability of Zn and copper hexacyanoferrates in neutral electrolytes results in poor Coulombic efficiencies and rapid capacity fading of AZIBs employing those materials as the cathode. 110 These issues are typically addressed by adding MnSO4 in the electrolyte (to prevent Mn2+ dissolution) or by adjusting the pH of the electrolytes to mildly acidic values (pH < 4 stabilizes Zn/CuHCFs). 20
,
104 Increase in electrolyte pH as a consequence of proton co-intercalation with Zn2+ could promote the dissolution of vanadium- and manganese-based oxide materials. However, if the pH change is buffered by hydroxide precipitation as the LDH (e.g., Zn4SO4(OH)6.5H2O, which precipitates at a pH ∼ 5.7 from a ZnSO4 electrolyte), proton intercalation can become the primary electrochemical reaction. 66 This occurs more frequently than disclosed in many reports; in fact, a common fallacy is to assume that Zn ions are the only charge carriers, when in fact they are usually also proton carriers. As a result, a large amount of co-formed insulating LDH will precipitate on the cathode, aggravating stability concerns. In large-format cells with high cathode loadings, the LDH may cause kinetic barriers rarely observed during small-scale investigations, as buildup of the LDH will lead to significantly increased impedance across the electrode-electrolyte interface.

Water electrolysis to release O2 at high voltages (>2 V against Zn) can also severely limit the utilization of high-voltage cathodes. This can be tackled by increasing the anodic stability of electrolytes through the use of extremely high salt concentrations. A more economical solution that does not affect other performance parameters, especially for highly reactive nanostructured cathode materials, will likely involve the application of electrolyte additives (e.g., surfactants such as SDS) that can adhere to the electrode surface and provide passivation and surface coatings.

Many (if not most) of the reported Zn-ion cathodes are based on oxides, which in aqueous media boast impressive cycle lives at fast rates; however, their relatively poor cyclability at slow rates indicates a significant contribution of capacitive behavior to reported capacities, rather than true Zn2+ storage (Figure 11A). Super-fast testing protocols are misleading, leading to yet another misconception that fast rate cyclability will translate to good low rate performance. Although we believe that the field is at too early a stage to recommend too many specific testing protocols, we emphasize that the areal capacity, current density, and electrode-electrode distance must be provided in the literature; they are very important in the context of Zn cycling, dendrite formation, and cathode longevity. Some other testing protocols can identify important issues. For example, resting after discharge influences the charge capacity that can be recovered and also provides a signal that H+/Zn2+ co-intercalation has occurred. Pauses in (dis)charge during cycling can help determine if good performance can be obtained at slow rates.

Such protocols are particularly important for practical applications of ZIBs, where the need for more advanced testing procedures will likely become evident as the field matures. Ultimately, whether or not Zn-ion will become realistic technology will depend to some degree on how low the cost will drop for Li-ion batteries, whether their recycling becomes a viable technology, and the extent to which Li-ion is adopted for vehicular e-mobility. Based on the safety aspect alone, however, Zn-ion technology will likely find applications. We believe that the low-voltage platform defined by the Zn anode would probably not offer the high energy densities that are needed for mobility applications (i.e., EVs and drones), and high capacities (within a realistic voltage range) are unlikely to be attainable. However, grid-storage and other stationary applications, such as replacements for car start-up batteries, home batteries, robots on wheels, or distributed power for telecommunication stations, could be viable applications (i.e., anywhere that lead acid is currently used and where safety is a prime concern). In assessing progress in the field, we note that much of the literature does not report cost analyses, corrosion performance, or gravimetric capacity—let alone system energy density or volumetric capacity—because the field is still in its infancy (unlike Li-ion). These are important metrics for future development, as elegantly outlined for a wide variety of alkaline Zn-battery systems. 149

From a fundamental point of view, an in-depth understanding of cathode-electrolyte compatibility, particularly concerning proton intercalation and O2 evolution is essential for host material selection and design in aqueous systems. Researchers are urged to limit ultrafast charging that only benefits from capacitive contributions, carry out careful physicochemical studies to distinguish between true Zn2+ intercalation and proton intercalation (i.e., ensure they have proof that capacity arises primarily from Zn2+ intercalation before assuming this), explore differences between Zn2+ intercalation from aqueous and non-aqueous media, and delve into the fundamental science to understand the role of de-solvation at the electrode interface. It is only through the identification of host chemistries and functionalities that trigger unwanted side reactions with aqueous electrolytes that it is possible to devise facile surface modifications and electrolyte optimizations toward a long-lasting AZIB. The most promising strategies are likely to be based on low-cost, environmentally friendly, elemental-abundant inorganic hosts that minimize or eliminate proton co-intercalation. We further note that despite a small (proportional) number of publications, organic cathode materials have already demonstrated impressive performance metrics such as cycle life (particularly at slow rates) and decent capacity (Figure 11B). Thus, they may be particularly promising candidates for the focus of future research and also for investigation in non-aqueous cells.

The beneficial effects of an abundant Zn anode hold true for non-aqueous systems as well. Although hindered diffusion of divalent Zn in most solid structures can limit the choice of active host materials, Zn[Ni1/2Mn1/2Co]O4 delivers the highest energy density yet reported for Zn-intercalation cathode material at an open circuit voltage of 2.05 V (305 Wh kg−1), 18 suggesting that new strategies involving spinel oxides and other related materials may show much promise. Nonetheless, complex non-aqueous Zn electrochemistry adds a layer of complication in identifying practical NZIBs. Although one solution to poor solid-state diffusion is rooted in implementing open framework structures with wide three-dimensionally interconnected pathways and chemistries with weaker electrostatics, a strategy to counter sluggish charge transfer kinetics in the non-aqueous system is yet to be found. Strong solvation and cation-anion pairing in non-aqueous electrolytes impede charge transfer at the solid-liquid interface and thus limits the overall electrochemical performance. Possible strategies include (1) electrolyte development to ensure stronger anion solvation that can further disrupt ion-pairing, (2) development of layered hosts with large interlayer spacings to accommodate Zn2+ together with its solvation sheath or utilizing open framework organic materials, and (3) tuning of cathode surface chemistries to minimize the charge transfer barrier at the electrode interface through strong bonding to incoming Zn2+.
